risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Computerized randomization within each hospital is carried out, with participants being stratified by gender, use of antidepressant medication, and their score on the BDI-II at baseline." Comment: The study explicitly states that allocation used computerized randomization with stratification, which is a proper random sequence generation method.

Quote: "Treatment allocation

Computerized randomization within each hospital is carried out, with participants being stratified by gender, use of antidepressant medication, and their score on the BDI-II at baseline." Comment: This describes how the random sequence/allocation was generated or implemented (computerized randomization and stratification), but it does not explicitly state any allocation concealment mechanism such as central randomization, pharmacy-controlled allocation, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes. Therefore, it is unclear whether the person enrolling participants could foresee the upcoming assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Prior to their randomization, patients are blinded to the study condition... After randomization the patients are given more precise information about the treatment they are going to receive." Comment: This indicates participants were only blinded before randomization and were informed of their assigned intervention afterward; personnel delivering CBT/MBCT would also necessarily know which treatment they were providing. The study includes outcomes highly susceptible to performance bias from knowledge of allocation, especially self-reported depressive symptoms (BDI-II), anxiety, well-being, diabetes-related distress, therapeutic alliance, and expectancy-related measures. Therefore lack of blinding after allocation could plausibly influence outcomes.

Quote: "After randomization the patients are given more precise information about the treatment they are going to receive." and "The primary outcome of the study is severity of depressive symptoms as assessed with the Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II)." and "Psychological secondary outcomes are generalized anxiety measured by the Generalized Anxiety Disorder scale (GAD-7), well-being measured by the Well-being Index (WHO-5), diabetes-related distress measured by the Problem Areas in Diabetes scale (PAID), and intersession changes in mood assessed by the Emotion Thermometers Tool (ETT)." Comment: Outcomes were assessed largely by patient self-report, so the patient is the outcome assessor for the most vulnerable outcomes. Participants were only blinded prior to randomization and then informed of their assigned treatment, meaning self-reported subjective outcomes were unblinded. Because the study includes multiple subjective self-reported outcomes (BDI-II, GAD-7, WHO-5, PAID, mood ratings), knowledge of allocation could bias outcome measurement. Although HbA1c from records is more objective, the worst-outcome rule applies, so the presence of unblinded subjective outcomes makes the overall domain High risk.

Summary: The supplied article text is a study protocol/design paper and does not report observed post-randomization attrition or per-arm numbers analysed at any outcome time point. It states only planned procedures and an anticipated dropout assumption: "Figure 1 illustrates participant recruitment and flow through the study" and "Allowing a drop-out rate of 25%, we are able to include 32 patients in each of our three conditions in completer analyses." Comment: This is not actual outcome data attrition. No per-arm randomized versus analysed numbers, no reasons for missing outcome data, and no observed follow-up proportions at T5/T6/T7 are reported, so attrition bias for the outcomes of interest cannot be judged as low or high from this paper.
